texts.label: text
texts.orig: During Li recovery from salar brines, Li concentration is typically increased to about 60,000 mg L -1 by evaporation. We investigated the concentration changes of Li, Na, K, Mg, Cl, SO4, and B during evaporation of both natural Uyuni and artificial Atacama brines. The Uyuni brine exhibited a maximum Li concentration of 6810 mg L -1 at 31 days of evaporation, at which point the majority of the Na and K in the brine was removed. The Li concentration decreased with further evaporation due to precipitation as Li2SO4, such that the level at the 56 day mark was approximately 4130 mg L -1 . In contrast, the artificial Atacama brine showed no pronounced Li precipitation, even after 54 days, at which point the Li concentration was 21,800 mg L -1 . The initial concentrations of Na and K in the Atacama brine were higher than those in the Uyuni brine, and the Atacama solution still retained K after 54 days of evaporation. The order of precipitation of cation species during the evaporation of both brines was: Na, followed by K, Mg, and Li. Thus, Li precipitation in the Atacama brine might be prevented due to the more favored precipitations of Na and K, such that significant Li removal did not occur in this brine.

texts.orig: Lithium is used in many applications, including the lithium-ion batteries which power mobile phones and the light aluminum alloys incorporated in modern airplanes. texts.label: text
texts.orig: The Salar de Uyuni, located in southeast Bolivia, is the world's largest salt lake at about 12,000 km 2 and has estimated Li resources totaling 10.2 Mt (Kesler et al ., 2012). However, the area within this salt lake in which the Li concentration is over 1000 mg L -1 is limited and the Li level in most of the lake is in the range of 500 to 600 mg L -1 (Tahil, 2008). In addition to its lower Li concentration compared to the Atacama salar brine, the solar evaporation rate here is less than half that at the Salar de Atacama (Tahil, 2008). Furthermore, other chemical components in the Salar de Uyuni brine make it difficult to recover Li (Tahil, 2008; Gruber & Medina, 2010; Gruber et al ., 2011; Kesler et al ., 2012). Salar brines with lower Mg/Li ratios are considered to be suitable for Li recovery, whereas the Mg/Li ratio of 18.6 in the Uyuni salar brine is about three times higher than the ratio in the Atacama salar brine (Tahil, 2008). Thus, manysalar brines which are expected to serve as future Li resources, including the Uyuni brine, do not have chemical compositions amenable to Li recovery, and in fact Li is not being recovered commercially from the Uyuni salar brine for this reason. As a result, Li recovery from brines with higher Mg/Li ratios is presently being studied (e.g., Hamzaoui et al ., 2003; An et al ., 2012).

As a result, Li recovery from brines with higher Mg/Li ratios is presently being studied (e.g., Hamzaoui et al ., 2003; An et al ., 2012).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Before Li is recovered as its carbonate from the Atacama salar brine, the Li concentration is increased to a level of 60,000 mg L -1 by solar evaporation. In this study, we performed evaporation experiments using naturally occurring Uyuni salar brine which had already been concentrated by a factor of approximately five times via exposure to solar evaporation. Our intent was to investigate whether the process currently used to recover Li from the Atacama salar brine could potentially be applied to brine from the Uyuni location, which has a significantly higher Mg/Li ratio. For comparison purposes, similar experiments were also performed using an artificial Atacama brine. During the evaporation process, we monitored the concentration changes of not only Li but also undesirable elements which may interfere with the Li recovery process, such as Mg and other major brine components including Na, K, Ca, B, Cl, and SO4.
texts.text: Before Li is recovered as its carbonate from the Atacama salar brine, the Li concentration is increased to a level of 60,000 mg L -1 by solar evaporation. In this study, we performed evaporation experiments using naturally occurring Uyuni salar brine which had already been concentrated by a factor of approximately five times via exposure to solar evaporation. Our intent was to investigate whether the process currently used to recover Li from the Atacama salar brine could potentially be applied to brine from the Uyuni location, which has a significantly higher Mg/Li ratio. For comparison purposes, similar experiments were also performed using an artificial Atacama brine. During the evaporation process, we monitored the concentration changes of not only Li but also undesirable elements which may interfere with the Li recovery process, such as Mg and other major brine components including Na, K, Ca, B, Cl, and SO4.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Throughout experimental trials involving evaporation, various brine components will be concentrated due to loss of water from the brine by evaporation while being simultaneously removed as the result of salt precipitations. During our study we were able to quantitatively estimate the salt concentrations in the brine by correcting for the degree of water loss. The aim of this study was to quantify the differences in the chemical reactions that occur during evaporation of the Uyuni and Atacama brines. It is widely accepted that the removal of Mg is necessary for Li recovery from brines (Tahil, 2008; Gruber & Medina, 2010; Gruber et al ., 2011; Kesler et al ., 2012). To the best of our knowledge, however, there have been no reports to date which describe the mechanism by which Mg interferes with the recovery process. Therefore, we were especially interested in clarifying the relationship between the behaviors of Li and Mg in the brine as well as the effects of any other coexisting components.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Two types of brine samples were prepared. One was Uyuni salar brine that had been concentrated by a factor of about five in a solar evaporation pond, and which had been sampled in August 2010 and sent to Japan. The other sample was an artificial brine prepared from reagent grade LiCl, NaCl, KCl, CaCl2, MgCl2·7H2O, MgSO4 and H3BO3. The chemical composition of the artificial brine was adjusted to match that of the Atacama salar brine used by the Sociedad Química Minera de Chile (SQM) for potassium chloride and lithium recovery (Muriate of potash: MOP), whose data are summarized in Abe and Ohno (2009).
texts.text: Two types of brine samples were prepared. One was Uyuni salar brine that had been concentrated by a factor of about five in a solar evaporation pond, and which had been sampled in August 2010 and sent to Japan. The other sample was an artificial brine prepared from reagent grade LiCl, NaCl, KCl, CaCl2, MgCl2·7H2O, MgSO4 and H3BO3. The chemical composition of the artificial brine was adjusted to match that of the Atacama salar brine used by the Sociedad Química Minera de Chile (SQM) for potassium chloride and lithium recovery (Muriate of potash: MOP), whose data are summarized in Abe and Ohno (2009).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In evaporation trials, a one-liter sample of each brine was transferred into separate one-liter beakers and evaporation of the brines in the beakers was promoted by air flow from a fan. Over an evaporation span of approximately two months, both brines were sampled five times at various intervals, at which times the sample masses were also measured.
texts.text: In evaporation trials, a one-liter sample of each brine was transferred into separate one-liter beakers and evaporation of the brines in the beakers was promoted by air flow from a fan. Over an evaporation span of approximately two months, both brines were sampled five times at various intervals, at which times the sample masses were also measured.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Aliquots taken from both brine batches during evaporation trials were first filtered through a 0.45 μ m membrane filter and then diluted by a factor of 1000. Inductively coupled plasma-atomic emission spectroscopy (ICP-AES, iCAP 6000, Thermo Fisher Scientific, Waltham, MA, USA) was used to quantify Li, B, Na, Mg, K and Ca in the samples, while SO4 was also quantified on the basis of the S content. Chloride ion contents were determined by ion chromatography (761, Metrohm, Herisau, Switzerland).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The initial concentrations of Li in the concentrated Uyuni and the artificial Atacama brines were 1820 and 3020 mg L -1 , respectively (Tables A1 and A2). The initial concentrations of Mg, which is an undesirable component with regard to Li recovery, were 40,500 mg L -1 for the Uyuni brine and 17,600 mg L -1 for the Atacama sample, such that the Uyuni brine Mg concentration was approximately 2.3 times that of the Atacama brine. In general, Na and K levels in the artificial Atacama brine were higher than those in the concentrated Uyuni sample, whereas the latter had a higher SO4 concentration.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3.2 Results of experimental trials with the concentrated Uyuni brine
texts.text: 3.2 Results of experimental trials with the concentrated Uyuni brine

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The mass of the Uyuni brine was observed to decrease up to 45 days, after which no further significant changes were found (Fig. 1). During the entire evaporation period, Cl and B were the only two components whose concentrations simply increased, going from 19,800 mg L -1 to 33,700 mg L -1 in the case of Cl and from 1720 mg L -1 to 11,400 mg L -1 for B. Conversely, the Na concentration decreased from 42,700 mg L -1 to undetectable levels while the Li concentration went from 1820 mg L -1 to 6810 mg L -1 during the first 31 days and then decreased to 4130 mg L -1 . The Mg concentration increased in a generally constant manner from 40,500 to 119,000 mg L -1 up to 45 days, although small concentration variations were observed between 14 and 31 days. Following the 45 day mark, the Mg level tended to decrease, attaining a final value of 106,000 mg L -1 . After reaching a maximum value at 14 days, the SO4 concentration fell back to almost its initial value. Similar to the trends observed with Mg and SO4, the K concentration exhibited a convex pattern, eventually dropping to an undetectable level. No discernable trends were noted in the case of the Ca levels.

texts.orig: Trends in the decreasing mass of the brine and the concentration changes of Na, K, Cl, and SO4 observed in the experiments using the artificial Atacama brine were similar to the results obtained with the Uyuni brine, although the K concentration did not decrease to the same extent. texts.label: text
texts.orig: In contrast, the concentration variations of Li, Mg, and B were clearly different from those in the Uyuni experiments. The Li level increased from 3020 mg L -1 to 21,800 mg L -1 during evaporation, while the Mg concentration increased from 17,600 mg L -1 to 55,500 mg L -1 during the first 20 days and then plateaued. During the first 30 days, the B level increased from 680 mg L -1 to 3680 mg L -1 , then decreased slightly to 3480 mg L -1 .

texts.orig: To have confidence in these data, we first have to determine whether or not B was precipitated during the evaporation experiments. We calculated the overall charges of cation and anion species removed from the brines, based on the N B values. Using B as an indication of species concentration should result in that a charge loss due to cations estimated from N B is equal to the charge loss due to anion species. As an example, in the case of the Uyuni brine at the 56 day mark, the N B values in Figure 3 show that the precipitation factors for Li, Na, K, Mg, Cl, and SO4 were 66, 100, 100, 61, 74, and 86%, respectively.

texts.orig: During evaporation of the Atacama brine, the B concentration was observed to decrease between 30 and 54 days, clearly showing that B precipitation occurred over that time span. Because the quantity of B removed from the brine by this process was small compared to the reduction in the other components (with the exception of Li), there was minimal effect on the calculated charge balances (Table A2). However, a quantitative assessment of the chemical reactions occurring during the 30 to 54 day period was challenging. Since cation losses were balanced by anion losses only up until the 30 day point, we were able to quantitatively analyze reactions using N B values over that earlier duration.

texts.orig: Na and K were the sole cations precipitated during the first 14 days of evaporation (Fig. 3a) and, during this same duration, 34% of the Cl in the brine was removed (Fig. 3b). These data indicate the precipitation of chloride, first in the form of NaCl, then by KCl. From Figure 2a, it is evident that these minerals were not hydrated. Figure 3 reveals that 58 and 44% of the Mg and SO4 remained in the brine at the 31 day mark, while almost all of the Na was precipitated and only 11% of the original K remained. Figure 2a clearly indicates the formation of hydrates during this span. The N Bs and charge balance data demonstrate the formation of NaCl, KCl, MgCl2 and MgSO4 during this phase, in estimated molar ratios of 1: 0.27:0.39:0.26.

texts.orig: Significant removal of SO4 was observed after 20 days and 40% of the Mg in the brine had been lost by the 30 day point. In general, the salts precipitating after 14 days of evaporation are believed to have been hydrates (Fig. 2b). At this point, the Na and K levels decreased to about 1.7 and 13%, respectively, and removal of anions also progressed such that the remaining Cl and SO4 levels are estimated to be about 26 and 55%. In contrast to the Uyuni trial, a small degree of Li removal could be observed. The N B values for these elements suggest the precipitation of NaCl, KCl, MgCl2, MgSO4, and Li2SO4 up to this point. However, as noted above, it is difficult to infer the precipitated Li phase from N Bs and charge balance calculations because of the low Li concentration compared to the other components.

texts.orig: 4.4 Differences in Li concentrations during the evaporations of the Uyuni and Atacama brines
texts.text: 4.4 Differences in Li concentrations during the evaporations of the Uyuni and Atacama brines

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Because the B in the artificial Atacama brine likely began to precipitate after 30 days (Fig. 2b), it is challenging to infer the Li behavior. However, taking into consideration the increase in the Li concentration of the Atacama brine to 21,800 mg L -1 (Table A2), it is likely that significant Li removal did not occur in this brine, as opposed to the Uyuni brine, in which Li was not effectively concentrated (Fig. 3a). The most significant difference between the two brines was that the initial Na and K concentrations in the artificial Atacama brine were higher than those in the concentrated Uyuni solution (Tables A1 and A2). The order in which the cation species precipitated during evaporation of both brines was: Na, followed by K, Mg, and Li (Figs 3a, 4a). As evaporation progressed, the Na and K in the concentrated Uyuni brine were nearly completely removed, following which the Mg and Li may have been alternately removed from the brine according to the phase diagram produced by Gao and Deng (2011). Hence the presence of Na and K in the brines may have hindered the alternating precipitation of the Mg and Li and in fact the high concentrations of Na and K in the artificial Atacama brine seem to have delayed the onset of salt precipitation. The NB values also allow us to confirm differences in the onsets of salt precipitations between the two brines. As an example, about 94% of the Na in the concentrated Uyuni brine was removed within the first 14 days of evaporation (Fig. 3a), whereas more than 30% of the original Na remained in the Atacama brine at the 13 day point (Fig. 4a). It is possible that this level of Na was sufficiently high so as to disallow the formation of a KCl precipitate. Threfore, significant amounts of Na may delay K precipitation (Fig. 4a) and the resulting higher concentration of K would in turn delay the removal of Mg, which in turn would hinder the precipitation of Li. The end consequence of this chain would result in a high Li concentration over a prolonged duration. Interestingly, K is used to promote the precipitation of Mg as carnalite (KMgCl3·6H2O) (Boryta et al ., 2011). Thus, large quantities of K in the brine might contribute not only to elevated Li concentrations but also to an elevated rate of Mg removal in the form of carnalite.
texts.text: Because the B in the artificial Atacama brine likely began to precipitate after 30 days (Fig. 2b), it is challenging to infer the Li behavior. However, taking into consideration the increase in the Li concentration of the Atacama brine to 21,800 mg L -1 (Table A2), it is likely that significant Li removal did not occur in this brine, as opposed to the Uyuni brine, in which Li was not effectively concentrated (Fig. 3a). The most significant difference between the two brines was that the initial Na and K concentrations in the artificial Atacama brine were higher than those in the concentrated Uyuni solution (Tables A1 and A2). The order in which the cation species precipitated during evaporation of both brines was: Na, followed by K, Mg, and Li (Figs 3a, 4a). As evaporation progressed, the Na and K in the concentrated Uyuni brine were nearly completely removed, following which the Mg and Li may have been alternately removed from the brine according to the phase diagram produced by Gao and Deng (2011). Hence the presence of Na and K in the brines may have hindered the alternating precipitation of the Mg and Li and in fact the high concentrations of Na and K in the artificial Atacama brine seem to have delayed the onset of salt precipitation. The NB values also allow us to confirm differences in the onsets of salt precipitations between the two brines. As an example, about 94% of the Na in the concentrated Uyuni brine was removed within the first 14 days of evaporation (Fig. 3a), whereas more than 30% of the original Na remained in the Atacama brine at the 13 day point (Fig. 4a). It is possible that this level of Na was sufficiently high so as to disallow the formation of a KCl precipitate. Threfore, significant amounts of Na may delay K precipitation (Fig. 4a) and the resulting higher concentration of K would in turn delay the removal of Mg, which in turn would hinder the precipitation of Li. The end consequence of this chain would result in a high Li concentration over a prolonged duration. Interestingly, K is used to promote the precipitation of Mg as carnalite (KMgCl3·6H2O) (Boryta et al ., 2011). Thus, large quantities of K in the brine might contribute not only to elevated Li concentrations but also to an elevated rate of Mg removal in the form of carnalite.
